1: ʾeśśāʾ ʿênay ʾel-hehārîm mēʾayin yābōʾ ʿezrî — "I lift my eyes toward the hills — but where does my help come from?" — the question mark is crucial; the verse is a genuine question, not an affirmation. The hills are not the source of help: the pagan high places (bāmôt) were located on hills, and lifting eyes to the hills might evoke idolatrous worship. The pilgrim raises the question deliberately — and immediately answers it in v2: my help is not from the hills but from the one who made them. The Ascents setting (the pilgrim traveling toward Jerusalem) lends this a pilgrimage urgency.
2: ʿezrî mēʿim yhwh ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ — "my help comes from the LORD, the maker of heaven and earth" — the answer to v1's question. ʿōśēh šāmayim wāʾāreṣ (maker of heaven and earth) — the same divine title as Psalms 115:15, Psalms 124:8. The hills are part of the created order (earth); the LORD made them. Therefore the one who made the hills is greater than the hills; looking to the hills and then to their Maker is the psalm's theological argument. The participle ʿōśēh (ongoing making/sustaining) suggests not just original creation but active maintenance.
3: ʾal-yittēn lammôṭ raglĕkā ʾal-yānûm šōmĕrekā — "he will not let your foot slip; your keeper will not slumber" — šōmēr (keeper, guardian, watchman) appears here for the first of six times in eight verses — a structural concentration unique in the Psalter. yānûm (to slumber, nod off, doze) — a light, momentary sleep. The distinction between nûm (doze) and yāšan (full sleep, v4) is deliberate: God neither dozes for a moment nor falls into full sleep. The foot not slipping is both literal (rocky Judean terrain on the ascent to Jerusalem) and metaphorical (moral stumbling in covenant life).
4: hinnēh lōʾ-yānûm wĕlōʾ yîšān šômēr yiśrāʾēl — "See — he who guards Israel will neither slumber nor sleep" — the emphatic particle hinnēh (behold, look!) draws attention. lōʾ-yānûm wĕlōʾ yîšān — the double negation with two different sleep-words is emphatic: neither momentary dozing nor full sleep. The transition from the individual ("your keeper," v3) to the corporate ("keeper of Israel") situates the personal assurance within the covenant people as a whole — this is the LORD who never took his eye off Israel through the wilderness, the judges period, and the exile.
5: yhwh šōmĕrekā yhwh ṣillĕkā ʿal-yad yĕmînĕkā — "the LORD is your keeper; the LORD is your shade at your right hand" — ṣēl (shade, shadow) — in the ancient Near Eastern sun, shade was not comfort but survival. The divine shade is a metaphor for protective covering (Psalms 91:1; Numbers 14:9 uses lack of shade for an unprotected enemy). ʿal-yad yĕmînĕkā (at your right hand) — the right hand is the position of the defender in battle (Psalms 16:8; Psalms 109:31). The LORD-as-shade stands at the right hand of the pilgrim, between them and the blazing threat.
6: yômām haššemeš lōʾ-yakkĕkāh wĕyārēaḥ ballaylāh — "the sun will not strike you by day, nor the moon by night" — nākāh (to strike, smite) — the verb used for the plagues of Egypt (Exodus 3:20). In the ancient world both solar heat and moonlight were thought to cause illness (sun-stroke, moon-stroke/lunacy). The psalm does not endorse these beliefs but uses them to frame a comprehensive protection: whatever threatens from above — day and night, sun and moon — the LORD's shade covers.
7: yhwh yišmāreĕkā mikkol-rāʿ yišmōr ʾet-napšĕkā — "the LORD will protect you from all evil; he will guard your very life/soul" — kol-rāʿ (all evil/harm) — the comprehensive scope: not merely physical dangers but every category of harm. napšĕkā (your life, your soul, your whole self) — nepeš here is not merely the spiritual soul but the whole living person. The LORD's keeping reaches to the core of the person, not merely the exterior.
8: yhwh yišmōr-ṣēʾtĕkā ûbôʾekā mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām — "the LORD will watch over your coming and going, now and forever" — ṣēʾtĕkā ûbôʾekā (your going out and your coming in) — a merism for all of life's movements: leaving and returning, the whole circuit of daily existence. Deuteronomy 28:6 uses the same formula for the covenant blessing ("blessed shall you be when you come in, and blessed when you go out"). The temporal span mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām (from now and unto eternity) matches Psalms 113:2 and Psalms 115:18 — closing the pilgrim psalm with the eschatological horizon of Hallel praise.
